text: Designing good REST APIs helps developers use your service effectively. First, use clear, noun-based endpoints like `/users` or `/orders`. Each endpoint should perform a specific action using standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE). Always return appropriate status codes—200 for success, 201 for created resources, 400 for bad requests, and 404 if something isn’t found. Versioning is essential; include a version number in the URL (e.g., `/v1/users`) so you can make changes without breaking existing users. Finally, write thorough documentation explaining each endpoint, its parameters, and example responses. This makes your API predictable and easy to integrate.
